coredata.dc:title: A dual-mode fluorometric and smartphone-based colorimetric sensor for cyanide detection using tungsten disulfide quantum dots and silver nanoparticles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Azizi, Neda
Hallaj, Tooba
Samadi, Naser
coredata.dc:description: In this study, tungsten disulfide quantum dots (WS2 QDs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were applied to establish a dual-mode fluorometric and smartphone-based colorimetric sensor for the determination of cyanide ions (CN⁻). We indicated that the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs was quenched by AgNPs as a result of the inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism. Moreover, the quenched fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs switched on again in the presence of CN⁻ due to the interaction between AgNPs and CN⁻, leading to destroy AgNPs, diminishing their yellow color and reducing their IFE on WS2 QDs. Based on these facts, we established a turn-off-on fluorometric (with a range of 0.5–30 and detection limits of 0.17 µM) and smartphone-based colorimetric sensor (with 5–500 µM and detection limits of 1.2 µM) for CN⁻ measurement. The sensor was exploited for CN⁻ analysis in water and food samples with satisfactory results. The established dual sensor benefits from both methods advantages such as the high sensitivity of fluorometric sensors as well as simple and visual detection of smartphone-based colorimetric sensors. Additionally, our developed smartphone-based colorimetric sensor can be used as a portable sensor for the sensitive, rapid and low-cost analysis of CN⁻ in the field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cyanide
Dual sensor
Smartphone-based sensor
WS2 QDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorometric and smartphone- based colorimetric detection assay of CN⁻
Characterization of WS2 QDs and AgNPs
The quenching effect of AgNPs on WS2 QDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of WS2 QDs, (C) Fluorescence spectrum of WS2 QDs at (a) λex= 280 nm, (b) 300 nm, (c) 320 nm, (d) 340 nm, (e) 360 nm, (f) 380 nm, (g) 400 nm.
(A) FT-IR spectrum, (B) EDS spectrum, and (C) XRD pattern of WS2 QDs.
TEM image of WS2 QDs in the presence of (A) AgNPs and (B) AgNPs and CN⁻.

(A) The fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs/AgNPs (blue bar) in the presence of 5 μM of various anions (orange bar), (B) The fluorescence spectra of (a) WS2 QDs, (b) WS2 QDs/ AgNPs, (c) WS2 QDs/AgNPs in the presence of CN⁻ (20 µM), (Inset is the solutions photo under UV light (365 nm)).

(A) Fluorescence spectra (Inset is the fluorometric method calibration curve) and (B) Absorbance spectra (Inset is the colorimetric calibration curve by spectrophotometer) of WS2 QDs/AgNPs in the presence of different concentrations of CN- under the optimum condition), (C) (a) Screen capture, (b) Figure analysis by the ColorAssist app of iPhone 11 and (c) Smartphone-based colorimetric method calibration curve of WS2 QDs/AgNPs in the presence of different concentrations of CN- under the optimum condition.
Comparison of the established sensor with some previously reported methods for CN- detection.
Tolerance limits of organic and inorganic interfering species in the detection of 10 µM CN-.
Fluorometric determination of CN- in almond and water samples.

head.ce:title: A dual-mode fluorometric and smartphone-based colorimetric sensor for cyanide detection using tungsten disulfide quantum dots and silver nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Urmia University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Research Medicine Institute, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia 5714783734, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Research Medicine Institute, Urmia University of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Research Medicine Institute, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia 5714783734, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, tungsten disulfide quantum dots (WS2 QDs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were applied to establish a dual-mode fluorometric and smartphone-based colorimetric sensor for the determination of cyanide ions (CN⁻). We indicated that the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs was quenched by AgNPs as a result of the inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism. Moreover, the quenched fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs switched on again in the presence of CN⁻ due to the interaction between AgNPs and CN⁻, leading to destroy AgNPs, diminishing their yellow color and reducing their IFE on WS2 QDs. Based on these facts, we established a turn-off-on fluorometric (with a range of 0.5–30 and detection limits of 0.17 µM) and smartphone-based colorimetric sensor (with 5–500 µM and detection limits of 1.2 µM) for CN⁻ measurement. The sensor was exploited for CN⁻ analysis in water and food samples with satisfactory results. The established dual sensor benefits from both methods advantages such as the high sensitivity of fluorometric sensors as well as simple and visual detection of smartphone-based colorimetric sensors. Additionally, our developed smartphone-based colorimetric sensor can be used as a portable sensor for the sensitive, rapid and low-cost analysis of CN⁻ in the field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) have been increasingly utilized in various fields of science due to their superior physicochemical properties (Freeman and Willner, 2012). Currently, tungsten disulfide QDs (WS2 QDs) belonging to the family of transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) have attracted considerable attention due to their strong optical matter interactions and rich physical properties (Yin et al., 2020). Zero-dimensional (0D) nanoscale WS2 QDs, that have the highest quantum yields (QYs) and the maximum photoluminescence (PL) emission energy at 1.94 eV, are derived from monolayer 2 dimensional WS2 nanosheets (Shen et al., 2018). The quantum confinement and edges effects, appeared as a result of reducing the dimension of nanosheets, leading to increasing the PL QYs of WS2 QDs. In addition, WS2 QDs have higher solubility, larger surface-to-volume ratio, and more active locations than 2D WS2 (Manikandan et al., 2019). Compared to traditional semiconductor QDs, WS2 QDs have several important advantages including low toxicity, simple synthesis methods, high chemical stability and low environmental hazard. The mentioned properties make WS2 QDs the proper candidate for the development of various sensors, catalysis and bioimaging methods (Cao et al., 2018; Rai et al., 2023; Xiao et al., 2018).

Several turn-on fluorometric sensors based on WS2 QDs and different quenchers such as MnO2 (Yan et al., 2020), Fe3+ (Singh et al., 2019) and graphene oxide (Das et al., 2020) have been designed for the detection of glutathione, lipoic acid and dopamine, respectively. However, to the best of our knowledge, no fluorometric sensor based on the interaction between WS2 QDs and AuNPs or AgNPs (as quenchers) has been reported yet.

Noble metal nanoparticles including Au and Ag nanoparticles (NPs) have been applied in various fields including the development of colorimetric and fluorometric probes due to their special physicochemical properties (Kareem et al., 2020). Localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) has been known as a famous property of Au and AgNPs. The position and intensity of the LSPR peak are dependent on various factors such as environmental conditions, size and shape of NPs, and so on. This feature has been used in the development of various colorimetric sensors (Amjadi et al., 2017; Hallaj et al., 2021a; Hallaj and Amjadi, 2019). Due to their high extinction coefficient and wide absorption spectrum in the visible region, they can be also utilized as an effective quencher with various fluorophores for the development of fluorometric sensors (Lee and Jun, 2019). For example, fluorometric sensors based on AgNPs and fluorophores such as BNQDs (Zhan et al., 2019), SiQDs (Liu et al., 2019), CQDs (Wang et al., 2018), GQDs (Mehata and Biswas, 2021) and N,S-GQDs (Chen et al., 2017) have been reported for detection of acetylcholinesterase (AChE), ClO⁻, H2S, H2O2 and CN⁻, respectively.

Furthermore, Au and AgNPs with various fluorophores such as CQDs (Liang et al., 2021; Salari and Hallaj, 2021; Wang et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2015) have been utilized to establish fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensors. In these sensors, two different methods are utilized for the detection of analytes at the same time, so they can take advantage of both techniques such as the high sensitivity of fluorimetry and visual detection of colorimetry (Salari and Hallaj, 2021). Additionally, more reliable results can be obtained with dual-mode sensors. Among dual sensors, fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensors have attracted more attention due to the close relationship between light absorption and fluorescence emission (Hallaj et al., 2021b; Zhou et al., 2021).

Recently, digital image-based colorimetry has attracted tremendous attention due to its special advantages. In this method, the color alteration of the solution containing the reagents and analytes is detected by devices such as smartphones. Many color identification applications can be installed on smartphones to convert color information to numerical values such as ColorAssist which only detect the wavelengths of three main colors: red, green, and blue (RGB) (Kong et al., 2019; Shrivas et al., 2020). Smartphone-based colorimetric methods are so simple, environmentally friendly, portable and low cost which has made them promising procedures in food and environmental analyses to determine various small molecules, pharmaceutical compounds and ions (Azizi et al., 2022; Shrivas et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2022).

Cyanide (CN⁻) is one of the most commonly hazardous anions which is extremely dangerous for humans because of attaching to Fe3+ of heme cofactors in a3 cytochrome. This function leads to disorder in its oxygen transport (Li et al., 2016). According to the World Health Organization (WHO), the amount of CN⁻ in drinking water with a concentration higher than 1.9 µM is harmful (Hu et al., 2020). Despite the toxicity, CN⁻ is widely used in industrial processes such as gold mining, mineral extraction, plastic production, electroplating, and many other fields, so 2.6 million tons of CN⁻ is released into the environment all over the world every year (Cheng et al., 2016). Thus, determination of trace amount of CN⁻ has attracted a lot of attention and several analytical methods including electrochemical (Lindsay and O’Hare, 2006), chromatographic (Ding and Wang, 2018), chemiluminescence (Vahid et al., 2019), fluorimetric (Chen et al., 2017; Munusamy et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2018), colorimetric methods (Padghan et al., 2021; Tahir Waseem et al., 2022) have been applied for its detection. Additionally, some the synthetic probes (with complicated synthesis methods) have been applied for development of smartphone-based colorimetric and fluorometric methods for the detection of CN-(Erdemir and Malkondu, 2020a; Majeed et al., 2022).

Herein, we studied the interaction between WS2 QDs and Ag and AuNPs for the first time. Then, we designed a dual-mode fluorimetric-smartphone-based colorimetric sensor with WS2 QDs as fluorophore and AgNPs as a colorimetric and quenching reagent for CN⁻ analysis. We chose Ag NPs (as colorimetric reagent and quencher) to design our dual sensor because of their intense absorption band at the visible region, the substantial spectral overlap between them and WS2 QDs, and the high reactivity of CN− toward Ag NPs. Based on these facts, we developed a sensitive, selective and rapid dual sensor to measure CN⁻ in water and food samples.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Philips EM 208, Germany) was applied to determine the size and shape of WS2 QDs and AgNPs. Fluorescence and UV/Vis spectra were recorded by (F-2500 spectrofluorimeter, Hitachi, Japan) and (SPEKOL 2000, Analytik Jena, Germany), respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR; AVATAR, Thermo) was employed to obtain FT-IR spectra. X-ray diffraction (XRD) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analyses were studied by (Siemens D 500) and (MIRA3 FEG-SEM), respectively.

L-cysteine, silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO42 H2O), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7), potassium cyanide (KCN) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). The food sample (bitter almonds) was obtained from a local market (Iran, Urmia) and Deionized water (DI water) was used throughout the experiments. Different concentrations of CN⁻ were prepared in alkaline conditions by diluting stock solution using KOH solution (pH=10).

According to the previous work (Singh et al., 2019), a simple hydrothermal method was applied for the preparation of WS2 QDs. First, 0.125 g of Na2WO42 H2O was solved with 12.5 mL DI water and the solution pH was adjusted to ∼4.0. Next, the solution was mixed with 25 mL DI water including 0.25 g of L-cysteine and sonicated for 10 min. After transferring the as-prepared solution into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, it was heated at 200 ºC for 24 h. Finally, it cooled down to room temperature and was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The obtained solution was kept at 4 ºC for further use.

AgNPs were prepared according to our previous study (Abolghasemi-Fakhri et al., 2021). Briefly, 50 mL aqueous solution of 4.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 AgNO3 and 2 mL aqueous solution of sodium citrate (1%) were mixed under vigorous stirring for 10 min at 80 ºC. Then, a 10 µL ice-cold solution of 0.1 mol L−1 NaBH4 was injected into the above solution and stirred for 30 min. The appearance of yellow color in the solution indicated the forming AgNPs that could be used after 24 h aging at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric and smartphone- based colorimetric detection assay of CN⁻
ce:section.ce:para: For fluorescence analysis, 300 µl of AgNPs, 50 µl of glycine buffer (0.1 mol L−1, pH=11.5), and a suitable amount of CN⁻ standard or sample solution were added to the test tube and then diluted to 1900 µL with distilled water. After 5 min incubation at room temperature, 100 µl of WS2 QDs were also transferred into the tube. The fluorescence spectra of solutions were recorded (λex= 320 nm) after another 5 min.

For the smartphone-based colorimetric assay of CN⁻, the experimental solutions (prepared by the above-mentioned procedure) were placed in a box with constant light and at a fixed distance (8 cm) from the phone. IPhone 11 smartphone, with a 12 MP camera, was placed in the constant place on the box as a detector. The colorimetric signal of solutions was measured by a color analysis application named the Color Assist software installed on the smartphone. This application uses the camera to capture Red, Green and Blue (RGB) color intensity and convert them to comprehensible values.

To extract CN⁻ from the bitter almonds, the sample was prepared according to a reported method with some modifications (Sun et al., 2018). At first, the bitter almonds were completely crushed. 2.7 g of the crushed almonds was mixed with 6 mL of DI water and stirred for 1 h. Then, 3 mL of NaOH (0.06 M) was added to the above solution under stirring for another 5 min. The mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min and the supernatant solution was filtered through a 0.22-µm syringe filter. Finally, an appropriate volume of the obtained solution was added into the test tubes and measured according to the general procedure.
For water samples, no pretreatment was necessary. An appropriate portion of each sample was used for analysis according to the general procedure.

It should be mentioned that spiked concentrations of CN⁻ (reported in Tables 3 and 4) were added into samples before preparation methods. During the real sample preparation and the measurement procedure, the CN- spiked concentrations were diluted and placed in the method linear range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of WS2 QDs and AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: WS2 QDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method from Na2WO42 H2O and L-cysteine as precursors. TEM image ( Fig. 1A) confirms the formation of spherical shape WS2 QDs with a size distribution of 5±2 nm. The optical properties of prepared QDs were studied by investigation of fluorescence and absorbance spectra (Fig. 1B). The fluorescence spectra demonstrated the maximum intensity of emission at 410 nm with λex=320 nm. Fluorescence emission peaks at the excitation range of λex=280–320 nm had no shift but the red shift happened at the excitation range of 340–400 nm. This dependence of emission on the excitation wavelength can be related to the size dispersion of quantum dots (Pallikkarathodi Mani and Cyriac, 2020). It should be mentioned that no change was observed in the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs even after 3 months (Fig. S1, λex=320 nm and λem=410 nm). The UV-Vis absorbance spectrum of WS2 QDs (Fig. 1C) shows strong absorbance in the UV region (<300 nm) and two peaks at ∼335 and 385 nm that can be attributed to optical transitions from the valence band to the conduction band and excitonic absorption of WS2 QDs (Singh et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2016).

The structural properties of WS2 QDs were investigated by FT-IR, EDS, and XRD analyses. As can be seen in Fig. 2A, FT-IR spectrum confirmed the presence of surface functional groups in the structure of WS2 QDs, including C-H/W-S, S-S, SO, CO, NC and N-H/O-H appeared around 670, 975, 1450, 1640, 2135 and 3485 cm−1, respectively (Najafi et al., 2023; Singh et al., 2019). The existence of W, S and C elements was investigated by EDS (Fig. 2B), and the percentages were obtained about 12.8%, 21.2%, and 65.9%, respectively. XRD pattern of WS2 QDs (Fig. 2C) shows four diffraction picks at 2θ= 32⁰, 33⁰, 34⁰ and 40⁰ referred to 004, 100, 101 and 103 planes of WS2 QDs, respectively. They represent the hexagonal phase lattice of the synthesized quantum dots (Hang et al., 2019).

The morphology of prepared AgNPs was studied by TEM. According to Fig. S2A, the shape of AgNPs is spherical with an average size of around 14±2 nm. Also, as shown in the UV-Vis spectrum of AgNPs (Fig. S2B), the surface plasmon resonance absorbance peak of AgNPs appeared at 420 nm.

We chose Au and Ag NPs (as colorimetric reagent and quencher) to design our dual sensor because of the intense absorption bands of Ag and AuNPs at the visible region, the substantial spectral overlap between them and WS2 QDs, and the high reactivity of CN− toward Au and AgNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quenching effect of AgNPs on WS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the potential of WS2 QDs and Ag and AuNPs for designing turn-off fluorometric sensors, we studied the spectral overlapping between the absorbance spectrum of AgNPs and AuNPs as quenchers and fluorescence spectrum of WS2 QDs, respectively (Fig. S3). The synthesis method and characterization of AuNPs were reported in ESI, Fig. S2. Concerning the higher spectral overlap between AgNPs and WS2 QDs, we suspected that AgNPs can act as the most efficient quencher for WS2 QDs. We investigated the quenching effect of Ag and AuNPs on the fluorescence emission of WS2 QDs (Fig. S4). The Stern-Volmer curves were plotted for both NPs, as can be seen in Table S1, the higher Ksv value was obtained for AgNPs (3 × 1010). So, we chose AgNPs as the final quencher of WS2 QDs for designing our sensor.

The quenching mechanism can be attributed to the fluorescence energy transfer (FRET) and/ or inner filter effect (IFE) between WS2 QDs and AgNPs. To determine the IFE role on the fluorescence quenching of WS2 QDs, the obtained Stern-Volmer plot was corrected according to the Eq. (1) (Icorr and Iobs: corrected and observed fluorescence intensity; Aex and Aem solution absorbance at λex and λem) (Amjadi et al., 2018).
(1) Icorr /Iobs = 10(0/5Aex + 0/5Aem)

Comparing the corrected and uncorrected Stern-Volmer plots and the slop significant diminishing in the corrected plot confirmed the main role of IFE in the quenching mechanism (Fig. S5B (b)). Moreover, the TEM image of WS2 QDs in the presence of AgNPs ( Fig. 3A) indicated the interaction between WS2 QDs and AgNPs, leading to the aggregation of NPs and the diminished fluorescence emission ( Fig. 4B).
In the following, WS2 QDs and AgNPs were applied to develop a dual fluorometric and colorimetric method for CN⁻ sensing.

Among various anions whose effect was studied on the quenched fluorescence of WS2 QDs by AgNPs (Fig. 4A), the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs significantly retrieved in the presence of CN⁻ (Fig. 4B).

It can be attributed to the interaction between AgNPs and CN⁻, leading to the destruction of AgNPs, diminishing their yellow color and reducing their IFE on WS2 QDs. According to the literature, in the presence of dissolved oxygen, AgNPs react with CN- resulting in the formation of [Ag(CN)2]- (Bhatt et al., 2022; Izadi et al., 2024). The following reaction shows the interaction between AgNPs and CN⁻ (Li et al., 2016).
(2) 4 AgNPs + 8CN⁻ + 2H2O + O2 → 4[Ag(CN)2]⁻ + 4OH⁻, Eº= 0.851 V, Kº= 9.4 × 1024

Indeed, there are lots of unoccupied orbitals on the surface of AgNPs that can act as electron pair acceptors from CN- as a donor group. The electron transfer process between CN- and AgNPs can become CN- to a reactive species that can easily interact with the solution-dissolved O2, producing oxygen-free radicals in the solution. The produced radicals can oxidize the Ag0 to Ag+ that it can combine with CN- and form [Ag (CN)n](n−1). On the other hand, Ag2O can form from the interaction between oxygen-free radicals and Ag+ (Bhatt et al., 2022; Izadi et al., 2024).

To confirm the above-mentioned mechanism, we studied the TEM images of WS2 QDs/ AgNPs and the LSPR peak of AgNPs in the presence and absence of CN⁻. As shown in the TEM image (Fig. 3B), the formed aggregations of WS2 QDs/AgNPs disappeared in the presence of CN⁻. Additionally, with the increasing amount of CN⁻ in the AgNPs solution, the absorbance intensity of the AgNPs LSPR peak decreased (Fig. S6). The results confirmed the interaction between AgNPs and CN⁻ and our assertion. Based on this fact, we established a dual sensor for CN⁻ sensing using WS2 QDs and AgNPs.

To achieve the highest analytical performance for the analysis of CN⁻, we optimized some effective factors such as incubation time, pH, temperature, amount of WS2 QDs and AgNPs. The obtained optimized condition (pH=11.5, 0.1 M of glycine buffer, incubation time=5 min at room temperature, 100 µl of WS2 QDs and 300 µl of AgNPs) was reported in ESI, Fig. S7. Under these conditions, the turned-off fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs/AgNPs increased with adding various concentrations of CN⁻ ions ( Fig. 5A). We found that there is a linear relationship between restored fluorescence intensity and CN⁻ concentration at the range of 0.5–30 µM. The method regression equation, ∆F=5.82 C+17.19, was indicated in Fig. 5 A (insert) (where ∆F=F-F0 is the difference between the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs/AgNPs in the presence (F) and absence (F0) of CN⁻ and C is the concentration of CN⁻). Moreover, the limit of detection, LOD (3σ) and the limit of quantification, LOQ (10σ) of the developed method were obtained to be 0.17 and 0.5 µM, respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD) % for five replicate detections of 5 µM CN⁻ was 1.1%.

In addition, based on the obvious color change of the solution from deep yellow to pale yellow in the presence of various concentrations of CN⁻ (Fig. 5B (a)), we developed a colorimetric method for the detection of CN⁻ not only by a spectrophotometer but also by a smartphone. The absorbance of WS2 QDs/AgNPs solution decreased by increasing the CN⁻ concentration and there was a linear relationship between them at the range of 2.5–40 µM (Fig. 5B (b)) with LOD (3σ) and LOQ (10σ) 0.9 and 2.7 µM. RSD% for five replicate detections of 5 µM CN⁻ of the developed method was obtained to be 3.4%.

Furthermore, the RGB signal measured by the smartphone linearly diminished by increasing the CN⁻ concentration at the range of 5–500 µM. The calibration curve of the smartphone-based sensor was indicated at Fig. 5C (c), in which the linear regression equation was ∆RGB = 43.6 log C - 7.8 (where ∆RGB=RGB-RGB0 is the difference between Red, Green and Blue color intensity of WS2 QDs/AgNPs in the presence (RGB) and absence (RGB0) of CN⁻ ions and C is the concentration of CN⁻). The LOD (3σ) and LOQ (10σ) of the developed method were calculated to be 1.2 and 3.6 µM, respectively. RSD% for five replicate detection of 10 µM CN⁻ by the developed method was 2.3%. The detection limits of developed fluorometric and colorimetric methods were lower than 1.9 µM (WHO’s acceptable value of CN- in drinking water).

The comparison of analytical performance between established dual-mode sensors and some previously reported methods ( Table 1 and S2) indicated the better or comparable detection limit of our method. Compared to the chemosensors with complicated synthesis methods (Chen et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2019) our synthesis method is so simple. Furthermore, the proposed dual sensor benefits from both method's advantages such as the high sensitivity of fluorometric sensors as well as the simple and visual detection of smartphone-based colorimetric sensors. Additionally, our developed smartphone-based colorimetric sensor can be used as a portable sensor for the sensitive, rapid and low-cost analysis of CN⁻ in the field.

The selectivity of the method for the determination of 10 µM CN⁻ was examined in the presence of various ions that can be found in the real samples. The tolerable concentration ratio of interfering species (with a relative error < 5%) is reported in Table 2. The results show the desirable selectivity of the dual-mode sensor for the determination of CN⁻ in water and food samples. The common concentration of most studied species in water and food samples is lower than their tolerable levels or is decreased to below the limits with diluting samples in a pretreatment step.

The selectivity of smartphone-based colorimetric sensors was also studied and observed as not an important signal in the absence of CN⁻ (Fig. S8). Therefore, it can be concluded that the prepared sensor has an outstanding selectivity for sensing CN⁻ in water and food samples.

The applicability of established dual sensor for CN⁻ detection was investigated in environmental and food analyses. For this purpose, fluorometric and smartphone-based colorimetric methods were used for monitoring CN⁻ in water and food samples. The achieved data are summarized in
Tables 3 and 4. The recoveries of CN⁻, obtained between 95% and 108%, proved the accuracy of this dual mode method for CN⁻ determination in practical applications. Student's t-test (the related equation was given in ESI) was applied to evaluate the accuracy of analyses. As can be seen in Tables 3 and 4, the obtained t-statistics are less than t-critical (t-critical =4.3 for n=2 and P = 0.05), confirming that the null hypothesis is true and there are no significant differences between added and found values. We also applied the spectrophotometric pyridine-barbituric acid standard method (Greenberg et al., 1992) for CN- determination in unspiked almond and water samples. The results indicated that CN- was not detected in the samples.

In summary, we exploited WS2 QDs and AgNPs to establish the simple and sensitive dual fluorometric and colorimetric sensor for the detection of CN⁻. AgNPs acted as a fluorometric quencher and colorimetric agent. We indicated that the fluorescence quenching of WS2 QDs by AgNPs was attributed to the IFE, stemming from the spectral overlap between the fluorescence spectrum of WS2 QDs and the UV–V is spectrum of AgNPs. We also found that the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs/AgNPs solution retrieved in the presence of CN⁻. It can be attributed to the interaction between AgNPs and CN⁻, leading to the formation [Ag(CN)2]⁻ and destroying AgNPs. As a result, the WS2 QDs/AgNPs solution color was also changed from deep yellow to pall yellow. Based on these facts, a turn-on fluorometric and a smartphone-based colorimetric sensor was established for the determination of CN⁻ in food and water samples with satisfactory results. The established dual sensor benefits from both methods' advantages such as the high sensitivity of fluorometric sensors as well as simple and visual detection of smartphone-based colorimetric sensors. Additionally, our developed smartphone-based colorimetric sensor can be used as a portable sensor for the sensitive, rapid, and low-cost analysis of CN⁻ in the field. However, there are some sophisticated methods for CN- assay that can be more sensitive than ours.

Azizi Neda: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Hallaj Tooba: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Samadi Naser: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2024.106081.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Abolghasemi-Fakhri et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive turn-off–on fluorometric sensor based on S,N co-doped carbon dots for environmental analysis of Hg(II) ion
Luminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Abolghasemi-Fakhri, Z., Hallaj, T., Amjadi, M., 2021. A sensitive turn-off–on fluorometric sensor based on S,N co-doped carbon dots for environmental analysis of Hg(II) ion. Luminescence 36, 1151–1158. https://doi.org/10.1002/bio.4040

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Amjadi et al., 2017
